case_study_name: Lakes from southeast of the Tibetan Plateau
summary: This regime shift occurred around 12.5 to 11.7 cal. Kyr BP in lakes of the southeast of the Tibetan Plateau. The main changes of diatom assemblages were characterized by the substantial shifts from small-sized planktonic-dominated Cyclotella group to the tychoplanktonic taxon. This shift is strongly linked with the abrupt temperature increase at the beginning of the Holocene.

case_study_name: Wildfires in the Sagebrush Biome, Western US
summary: This regime shift is currently (2023) happening in the United States. The ecosystem is suspected to be shifting to a more frequent fire regime, thus permanently changing the native floras. This shift induces costs of fire suppression as well as the ecosystem services that can affect people's health and survival, recreational and cultural resources
impacts_on_regulating_services: Air quality regulation, Regulation of soil erosion

summary: This regime shift occurred from 2014 to 2017 on the Great Barrier Reef in Lizard Island, Australia. It stared by the severe tropical cyclones Ita and ended by mass bleaching events. The losses of coral were not uniform. In overall the coral cover declined from 36% to 7%.

key_direct_drivers: External inputs (e.g. fertilizers, pest control, irrigation), Global climate change

This shift contributed to the coral transition in the area.

case_study_name: Rio Grande del Norte National Monument, New Mexico, USA
summary: This regime shift is occurring since 1881 (begging of the analysis) in the Rio Grande del Norte National Monument, New Mexico, USA. The analysis was conducted in 2019 and highlighted the change in shrub and grass density since 1881. The ecosystem is experiencing an increase of sagebrush and a decrease of grass presence. The overgrazing and climate change were probably the main drivers of this shift to a high shrub presence. The newly established shrub could be more resistant to climate change and persist due to deeper roots that can access more water.
key_direct_drivers: Global climate change, Vegetation conversion and habitat fragmentation

case_study_name: Fire regime in British Columbia, Canada
summary: This regime shift occurred in the mid-2000s (around 2005) in British Columbia, Canada. This region experienced more frequent intense wildfires due to the rapid warming. summary: This regime shift likely started around 2009 and may continue for another 10 or 20 years in Macquarie Island, Australia. The Azorella macquariensis median cover dropped from 40 % in 2009 to 27 % in 2016. The north experienced an higher coverage reduction and dieback percentage. The presence of seedlings is encouraging but it is possible that the recovery will be slow and patchy.
This regime shift occurred around 1960 in the East China Sea coast, China. There was a change in phytoplankton productivity and a shift in community structure.
key_direct_drivers: External inputs (e.g. fertilizers, pest control, irrigation)

case_study_name: Sugar Kelp and Turf Algae in Norway
summary: This regime shift may have occurred in southern Norway around year 2000. It is still unclear if this shift could be reversible as the sugar kelp have a high dispersal potential and colonization rate. Climate change and eutrophication are two drivers of this shift
key_direct_drivers: External inputs (e.g. fertilizers, pest control, irrigation), Global climate change

case_study_name: Northern Gulf of Mexico, Louisiana, US
summary: This shift occurred at the end of the XX century (around 1978-1995) in the coastal waters of Louisiana, US. The seasonal hypoxia became more frequent, thus having negative impacts on the marine ecosystem.
key_direct_drivers: External inputs (e.g. fertilizers, pest control, irrigation)

summary: This regime shift occurred between February and March 1999 on the Hasaki coast, Kamisu, Japan. The morphology of the coast changed and the foreshore (beach) experienced accretion while the inner transition zone (submerged area) did not experienced change. This change was partially due to large-scale ocean-atmospheric patterns such as El NiÃ±oâsouthern oscillation and Arctic oscillation.

This regime shift occurred between October and November 2006 on the Hasaki coast, Kamisu, Japan. The morphology of the coast changed and the foreshore (beach) and the inner transition zone (submerged area) both experienced erosion. This change was interpreted as the result of large offshore wave energy fluxes.

This regime shift occurred between February and March 2008 on the Hasaki coast, Kamisu, Japan. The morphology of the coast changed and the foreshore (beach) experienced accretion while the inner transition zone (submerged area) experienced erosion. This change was interpreted as the result of the previous erosion which created a steeper slope, thus leading to more accretion on the foreshore. This later stage has been the last recorded in the study (2014).

case_study_name: Sediment Disposals in Estuarine Channels, Netherlands
summary: Sediment disposals in the two-channel estuariy of Western Scheldt, Netherlands, is altering the eco morphology of the intertidal flat. This disposal is due to the channel been deepened and the sediment relocated at another place.

case_study_name: Coral transition in Magnetic, Queensland, Australia
summary: This coral transition occurred from 2004 to 2012 on the Island Magnetic in the Queensland, Australia. The coral cover decreased from 40% to 21% while the macroalgae increased from 12% to 37% (figure 3 in the article).

case_study_name: Coral transition in Keppel Island, Queensland, Australia
summary: This coral transition occurred from 2005 to 2015 on Keppel Island in the Queensland, Australia. The coral cover decreased from 50-65% to around 30% while the macroalgae increased from 0-20% to 30-50% (figure 3 in the article).

case_study_name: Vegetation change in the northern part of Pechengsky District, Russia
summary: This regime shift occurred between 1930 and 2016, dates of the two vegetation surveys. It occurred in the northern part of Pechengsky District, Russia. The drivers were the climate change and the land use change (land abandonment).

case_study_name: Dreissenid mussel invasion, Lake Michigan, US
summary: This regime shift occurred in the 1990s as Lake Michigan, US, undergone a food web shift following the dressenid mussel invasion. At the same time, management policies were active to reduce the mercury loading in the lake. This food web shift led to a lower mercury reduction in the lake.

case_study_name: Chlorophyll decline in the North Sea
summary: This regime shift might have occurred around 2000 in the North Sea. A decline in annual mean chlorophyll was observed in 11 out of 18 sampling sites from 1988 to 2016. This may be the result of de-eutrophication due to the reduction of nutrients in rivers and the increasing surface sea temperatures.
key_direct_drivers: Global climate change, External inputs (e.g. fertilizers, pest control, irrigation)

summary: From 2000 to 2010, approximately 35% of Iran lands changed, mostly being desertification leading to the loss of shrub and grass.

case_study_name: Arctic zooplankton composition shift in Disko Bay, Western Greenland
summary: This regime shift occurred approximately from 2000 to 2007 in Disko Bay, Greenland. The zooplankton community shifted from being equally composed of small North Atlantic species (Calanus ï¬nmarchicus) and larger Arctic species (Calanus hyperboreus and Calanus glacialis) to being dominated by the small North Atlantic species (Calanus ï¬nmarchicus). Because of this shift, the lipid content of Calanus females decreased by 34%. Those changes will likely have an impact on the Arctic food web due to the prominent role of Calanus.

case_study_name: Shift toward non-canopy algae, south-western Australia
summary: This shift occurred between 2006 and 2015 (survey years) in the northern part of the south-western Australia. The density of algal species such as Ecklonia radiata decreased, while turf algae species increased.

case_study_name: Oyster fisheries collapse in Cape Cod Bay, US
summary: This regime shift occurred in Cape Cod Bay, US. This date correspond to the earliest year of reef degradation, derived from several indicators (landings data, importation of nonendemic oysters to restock, and earliest use of dredges). This is part of a series of regime shifts starting close to the urban center and spread down along the coast as the fisheries were collapsing and oysters from more distant estuaries were fished.

case_study_name: Fish community shift in eastern English Channel
summary: This regime shift occurred in the late 1990s in the eastern English Channel. Pelagic species with rapid life history cycles, characterized by broadcast spawning, small offspring size, and early maturation, declined in abundance. This followed an increase in sea surface temperature due to the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation combined with historical fishing pressure. During the same period, species with late maturation, high parental care, and few, well-developed offspring increased in abundance.
key_direct_drivers: Global climate change, Harvest and resource consumption

case_study_name: Ichthyoplankton assemblage shift in southern California, US
summary: This regime shift occurred in 1965 across spring and summer in southern California, US. The shift was associated with an increase from low to moderate abundance of several offshore taxa, as well as peaks in the abundance of Sebastes spp.

case_study_name: Ichthyoplankton assemblage shift in southern California, US
summary: This regime shift occurred in 1976 across spring and summer in southern California, US. The shift was associated with an increase in a large group of taxa characteristic of southern and offshore environments.

case_study_name: Ichthyoplankton assemblage shift in southern California, US
summary: This regime shift occurred in 1983 during spring in southern California, US. The shift appears to have been associated with variations in just a few highly abundant taxa. The 1983 spring assemblage regime shift has less evidence.

case_study_name: Ichthyoplankton assemblage shift in southern California, US
summary: This regime shift occurred in 1990 during spring in southern California, US. The shift appears to have been associated with variations in just a few highly abundant taxa.

case_study_name: Black-legged kittiwakes and Iberian sardines collapse in Sisargas Islands, Spain
summary: This regime shift occurred from 1991 to 1993 in Sisargas Islands, Spain. The sardine's population collapsed in 1991 and the population of their predator, the black-legged kittiwakes, collapsed 2 years later in 1993 due to the earlier sardine population collapse.

case_study_name: Coral transition in Lizard Island, Great Barrier Reef, Australia
summary: This coral transition started in 1996 in Lizard Island, (Great Barrier Reef), Australia. The first decline of 1996 decreased the coral cover to about 60 % of its original value. The coral cover then recovered to around 85 % of its original value in 2010 and then started to decline again.

case_study_name: Coral transition in Moorea, French Polynesia
summary: This coral transition started in 1980 in Moorea, French Polynesia. The coral cover is since then experiencing periodic declines and recoveries.

case_study_name: Coral transition in Discovery Bay, Jamaica
summary: This coral transition occurred from 1980 to 1990 in Discovery Bay, Jamaica. The coral cover was divided by 10 during those 10 years. Since 1990 the coral cover partially recovered, passing from 10 % to 20 % (2012) of its original cover.

case_study_name: Impact of dams on the southern Brazilian Atlantic Forest
summary: This regime shift occurred between 2010 and 2020 in the southern Brazilian Atlantic Forest. Due to the dam construction, the forest along the riverbanks lost habitat (flooding). The loss of habitats led to a shift in the species composition of communities.

The lake eutrophication of lake Dianchi in China occurred in the 90s due to higher nutrient inputs, destruction of submerged plants and biological invasion. At the time of the study (2024), the modelization indicates that the lake environmental variables are close to the recovery points. Nevertheless, those variables are highly sensitive to a reducing water level or increasing water inflow.

case_study_name: Barents Sea Ice loss, Arctic Ocean
summary: The loss of fresh, seasonal sea ice melt south of the Polar Front and shifts in the salinity of inflowing Atlantic Water have led to a notable rise in the salinity and density of Barents Sea Water since 2005. This change has significantly diminished the seasonal variation in sea ice area in the region, decreasing from 77,000 kmÂ² between 1985 and 2004 to just 8,700 kmÂ² between 2005 and 2016. Prior to 2005, sea ice dynamics in the eastern Barents Sea helped buffer Barents Sea Water against fluctuations in Arctic Water temperatures, but this buffering effect has weakened since 2005, allowing for increased temperatures in Barents Sea Water.

case_study_name: Decrease of a Carcinus maenas (green crabs) population, Minas Basin, Canada
summary: A ten-year monitoring program of green crabs (Carcinus maenas) in the Minas Basin of the Bay of Fundy, Canada, revealed significant changes starting in 2013. During that year, intertidal crab densities, average sizes, subtidal catch rates, and the proportions of mature and reproducing crabs all fell to record lows, with large crabs nearly disappearing. Although the population showed some recovery from 2014 to 2017, it remained in a modified state. While a physical oceanographic or climatic event around 2013 may have impacted the marine biota, its precise nature is uncertain. Despite the partial recovery, the characteristics of the crab population have distinctly changed.
observations: speculative regime shift, decrease of pop, shift inside the pop and a slow return to normal, but no long enough study

case_study_name: Declines in regional fish populations in coastal southern California
summary: Between 1977 and 2002, many coastal species off southern California experienced significant declines, coinciding with a regional climate shift. This climatic change represents a major, natural disturbance. Similar trends were observed across all locations within the Bight, indicating a regional decline in species abundance rather than a redistribution of individuals. These patterns suggest that the decrease in regional fish stocks is linked to reduced productivity due to the shift to a different climatic regime.

case_study_name: Sea urchin overgrazing - British Columbia, Gulf of Alaska
summary: A notable regime shift has occurred in temperate rocky reefs where sea urchin overgrazing has transformed vibrant macroalgal beds into barren urchin-dominated areas. This catastrophic shift, marked by a significant and difficult-to-reverse change, shows that recovering macroalgal beds requires drastically reducing sea urchin populations. Data from 13 global rocky reef systems reveal a consistent pattern of this shift, with a substantial gap in sea urchin biomass needed to revert to the original state. Human stressors worsen the situation by reducing macroalgal resilience and boosting the persistence of urchin barrens.
key_direct_drivers: Global climate change, Harvest and resource consumption, Environmental shocks (e.g. fire, floods, droughts)
threshold_variable: Biomass of sea urchins required to form barrens
observations: a lot of interesting information about thresholds and key drivers of kelp transitions Regime Shift

Data from 13 global rocky reef systems reveal a consistent pattern of this shift, with a substantial gap in sea urchin biomass needed to revert to the original state. Human stressors worsen the situation by reducing macroalgal resilience and boosting the persistence of urchin barrens.
key_direct_drivers: Global climate change, Harvest and resource consumption, Environmental shocks (e.g. fire, floods, droughts)
threshold_variable: Biomass of sea urchins required to form barrens
observations: a lot of interesting information about thresholds and key drivers of kelp transitions Regime Shift

summary: Urban sprawl means low-density expansion of big urban areas, and is a process that has existed in Europe since the mid 50's, when most of European cities were a lot more compact. This sprawling is very important because of the major impacts in increased energy and soil consumption, and land-use. Here is the example of sprawl along the RhÃ´ne corridor, transport axes and coastlines in the south of France, and more specifically of Lyon, one of the biggest cities of France
observations: There are a lot of cases studies in the article, if someone has time you can add them easily

case_study_name: Encroachment of forests into tundra landscapes, Noatak National Preserve, Northwest Alaska
summary: In vast areas dominated by tundra ecosystem, relatively little expansion of forests (mainly white spruce) occures because of the warming of temperature in Alaska. Since 1850 (to the year of the study in 1999) In the Noatak National Preserve, boreal forests treeline has gained 80 to 100 meters on the tundra. The germination and establishment of white spruce seedlings in Alaska benefit from rising temperatures, decreased competition from other plants, and maybe also more nutrient. But this treeline is supposed to continue to expand only if a larger climatic change (than the one in 1850-2000 years) occure.
observations: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-005-5352-2 : There are a lot of cases studies (not only on tundra to forests but also thermokarst lakes and others...) in this article, if someone has time you can add them easily
